paragraphText: Okay, it's been recording, so this is super helpful because we are just interested in talking to people who have been where these students are now and learning about how that experience was for you and what you are doing in life now.
But reference in your research personally Ortega I am 15 years old and from Michoacán.
I am from Oaxaca and I am 15 years old.
What's your name?Ah, my name is Laura Gómez.How old are you?Ah, I am 22 years old.
What is your father's name?My father's name is Alfredo Gómez.
What is your mother's name?
Esther Ortega?
Ah.
Do you have siblings?
Yes, I have two siblings.
I have a twin sister named Luz Gómez and a younger brother who is 16 years old and named Jesús.
Are you happy where you live?
Ah, yes, I am happy where I live.
Do you live alone with your parents?
I live with my parents at the moment.
Would you like to travel to a new place?
Yes, I would like to travel to a new place, but right now it is a little difficult due to political circumstances and also my immigration status.
What is your favorite pastime?
My favorite pastime right now is watching movies, and I am also doing some embroidery.
It's something that excites me.
Do you still have direct contact with your friends from Mexico?
No, I have only seen them a little sometimes when I go to my family’s parties, but I have not had direct contact with them since GMN from Mexico.
Can you tell me about a time when you felt happy at Pescadero middle school?
That’s going to be a little difficult because middle school was a bit hard for me.
I didn't want to speak English, so I didn’t have a strong connection with my classmates because I was embarrassed to speak English.
But one good experience was finding one of my best friends, who is still one of my best friends to this day.
What was the main reason for your decision to immigrate to the United States?
I didn’t have the option to immigrate.
My parents wanted me to come.
My mom came when I was five years old to follow my dad, and then when I was ten, my mom and dad called my grandparents to bring me here.
But it wasn’t a decision I wanted to make because I didn’t want to leave; I was just used to that world.
Tell me about a time when you felt really sad during this process of moving to another country.
I felt really sad when I was separated from my twin sister because we were always together.
When we were in Tijuana, they had to separate us, and I felt very sad because she wasn’t with me, and we had never been apart before.
Can you tell me in detail about how you crossed into this country?
So then, well, I arrived in Tijuana—I don’t remember when—but I arrived in Tijuana with, I think, my kindergarten godfather and my uncle.
We got to Tijuana and stayed in a hotel.

Then we went to get clothes, and after getting clothes, they took us to a woman’s house—I don’t remember her name—but she gave us food and told us, “Oh, you’re going to cross into the United States.” After that, they took me to another woman who took care of me for a few days, and then, literally, we just crossed the border.
Honestly, my mind has erased parts of it because I didn’t want to remember it and still don’t want to remember it.
Tell me a funny or sad story that happened to you at Pescadero school.
A really funny story for me now is that when I was in school, my friend would talk to me in English to make me laugh, but I didn’t know English.
So she would just be talking, and I would just stare at her.
I think I asked her the other day why she talked to me in English, and she said, “Oh, it was to make you laugh,” but I didn’t find it funny because I didn’t understand anything.
How did you handle the pressure of adapting to a new education system while also trying to fit in with your classmates?
Could you repeat the question?
Sorry.
How did you handle the pressure of adapting to a new education system while also trying to fit in with your classmates?

Well, I tried to focus on education for my dad and my parents because I saw them working in the fields, so I focused on the fact that I had to do things so I could at least finish high school since my parents didn’t finish.
But it was difficult.
I remember an English class I had with a teacher named Mr. Choy.
The other teachers would give me a pass and say, “It’s okay, you just got here, so I’ll treat you differently.” But he didn’t treat me differently.
He treated me like the other students.
So if I wrote an essay and it wasn’t good—if I didn’t write it well in English—he would tell me.
I think he was the first teacher to give me an F. With him, I learned that it didn’t matter what system I came from in Mexico; here, they weren’t going to give me a free pass.
It was hard to fit in with the kids because, like I said, I didn’t want to speak English when I was younger.
I was embarrassed about my accent, so I didn’t speak it at all.
I think I only started speaking it when I was in high school.
That’s why I tell others to speak English, because if you don’t, you’re going to have a really, really hard time.
But yeah, I think I fit in—it’s kind of funny now—but I fit in with the kids because I had Cheetos.
Back then, Cheetos were really popular, so that’s how I connected with them.
How did your family adapt to life in the United States?
Honestly, I’m not sure because, as I mentioned, my family was already living here before I did.

I think they’re still adapting because there are many cultural things they’re adjusting to in the U.S., like going to meetings, understanding the education system, the curriculum, the graduation requirements, and how colleges work, scholarships—things that are very different from Mexico.
Did you find any resources or support systems that helped you succeed academically or personally in school?
Ah, yes.
I think Puente was a great help to me because when I needed tutoring, they provided it.
Especially Liz—whenever I needed something, she helped me.
But in high school, in La Merced, they didn’t help much.
I had an English class, but it was only for a very short time, maybe an hour.
I think at Pescadero, La Merced didn’t help me that much, to be honest.
Tell me about how you live now as an adult.
My adult life is a little different from what I expected.
After I finished college in June, I planned to move, but to save money, I stayed with my parents.
Now I’m working as a nanny because I don’t have good insurance, and since my field is education, you usually need good insurance to teach children and be in the education system.
So, things have been a little more complicated than I thought.
When I was a senior in high school, the counselor told me, “Things are going to change when you leave high school, you’ll see,” and while not everything has changed, she was right.
But one thing they can’t take away from me is my education and my degree.
How did migrating to the U.S. change your goals and aspirations for the future?
When I was little in Mexico, my only dream was to hear my name called on the town speaker for a mom with kids, to have a married life.
That was my dream.
But when I came to the U.S., everything changed—kids, marriage, all of that moved to the bottom of my priorities.
Going to school became the most important thing to me.
What inspired you to study what you did?
The kids.
I took a lot of classes in college—anthropology, science, music, political science—but nothing interested me more than education.
Well, that concludes our interview for today.
Thank you.
Sorry about that.
Yes, we’re good.
Thank you.
Yes, thank you.
See you later, have a great day.
Likewise!
